Diagnosis:
Fetal MRI performed at 22 weeks gestation for further evaluation of suspected central nervous system anomalies on obstetric ultrasound. The examination reveals complete absence of the corpus callosum with characteristic teardrop-shaped configuration of the lateral ventricles (colpocephaly). The third ventricle is superiorly displaced. The atrium of the left lateral ventricle measures 12.5 mm and the right measures 13.1 mm, indicating moderate bilateral ventriculomegaly. The cerebral sulcation and migration patterns are otherwise appropriate for this early gestational stage.

Impression: Complete agenesis of the corpus callosum with associated moderate bilateral ventriculomegaly (colpocephaly). No additional intracranial or extracranial structural anomalies are identified on this study. Pediatric neurosurgery and genetics counseling are recommended.

Multiplanar, multisequence brain MRI reveals age-appropriate myelination of the white matter tracts without any developmental delay. There is no evidence of intracranial mass lesion, acute territorial infarction, or hemorrhage. The ventricular system, basal cisterns, and subarachnoid spaces are normal in size and morphology. The major intracranial arterial flow voids are preserved, and the craniovertebral junction is unremarkable.

Impression: Unremarkable and completely normal brain MRI for age. No structural etiology identified to account for the patient's new-onset seizures, which may warrant further electroencephalographic correlation.

Cardiac MRI performed to evaluate for myocarditis following a history of atypical chest pain and elevated troponins. Cine imaging demonstrates normal biventricular size, volume, and systolic function. The left ventricular end-diastolic volume index is 74 mL/m² with a normal ejection fraction of 62%. The right ventricular ejection fraction is 58%. Multiplanar T2-weighted and T2-mapping sequences show no evidence of myocardial edema. Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) imaging shows no focal subepicardial or mid-myocardial hyperenhancement to suggest myocardial necrosis or fibrosis.

Impression: Normal cardiac MRI with no evidence of active myocarditis, myocardial edema, or late gadolinium enhancement. Biventricular size and global systolic function are completely within normal limits.

Fetal MRI evaluated at 32 weeks gestation to assess fetal growth and anatomy. The cerebral hemispheres exhibit normal cortical folding and sulcation development appropriate for 32 weeks of gestation. The ventricular system is normal in size and symmetric, with the bilateral atrial widths measuring approximately 5.8 mm. The corpus callosum, cerebellar hemispheres, vermis, and brainstem appear completely intact and well-developed for this gestational age. Visualized fetal chest, abdomen, spine, and extremity structures are structurally normal with no evidence of diaphragmatic hernia or mass effect.

Impression: Normal fetal MRI study at 32 weeks gestation with no demonstrable intracranial, spinal, or visceral anomalies. Fetal brain maturation and sulcation pattern are entirely appropriate for the stated gestational age.

Cardiac MRI evaluation of a 12-year-old male with suspected aortic coarctation. Three-dimensional MR angiography demonstrates focal, severe narrowing of the proximal descending thoracic aorta immediately distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery. The minimum luminal diameter at the coarctation site measures 4.2 mm, compared to a normal distal transverse arch diameter of 11.5 mm. Marked collateral vessel development is visualized, including prominent internal mammary and intercostal arteries. Left ventricular mass is mildly increased at 85 g/m², suggesting early concentric hypertrophy in response to pressure overload.

Impression: Severe focal coarctation of the descending thoracic aorta with significant collateral circulation and mild secondary left ventricular hypertrophy. Left ventricular systolic function remains preserved with an ejection fraction of 64%.

MRI of the brain and cervical spine transition reveals a 9 mm downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils below the level of the foramen magnum. There is complete effacement of the CSF spaces at the craniovertebral junction. The ventricles are normal in size and configuration, and no acute intra-axial pathology is noted within the cerebral hemispheres. Cervical spine sagittal sequences demonstrate a small, non-tension syrinx within the mid-cervical spinal cord extending from C3 to C5.

Impression: Chiari I malformation with cerebellar tonsillar ectopia measuring 9 mm, associated with a small mid-cervical syrinx. Neurosurgical evaluation is recommended for correlation with clinical symptoms such as occipital headache or extremity paresthesia.
